titleStmt.title: Letter from Patrick J. Sullivan to Robert Chalmers, 10 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Patrick J. Sullivan

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Patrick J. Sullivan (1858 - 1937), to the Under-Secretary, Robert Chalmers (1858-1938). Sullivan writes to propose a Bill that holds a cure for dissension and disaffection in Ireland. Although Sullivan is in favour of the union between Ireland and Britain he wants a union that promotes the best interests of both. After the Easter Rising of 1916 Chalmers was sent to Ireland as under-secretary, replacing Sir Matthew Nathan (1862 - 1939), who had resigned along with his chief-secretary Augustine Birrell (1850 - 1933).

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/9855/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
7678 Knockaville, Mullingar 10 June 1916 The Under Secretary Dublin Castle. Sir, With respect to the foregoing file
I feel regret the appointments should
have been delayed, they would I do think
acting with me have put the proper day
light upon the most irritable questions
of the time so far as Ireland is concerned
and set aside almost every reasonable
cause of grievances existing in Ireland
and pass over very many mortal things
which have taken place in our midst. It is said somebody is to blame well I
am not going to say who is to be blamed
but you finding the opponents to what I
say or recommend so far as Ireland
is concerned and you shall find those
who are to blame. I am annexing a Bill of Settlement which
I am sure shall appear to you as a con-
glomeration of many things but in every
subject all the same there is a cure for
dissention and disaffection, and down
right misunderstandings. Of course on the
Bill I am open to criticism and the
Bill to amendment, but this is all. You take the Bill as it stands and you
save the time of Parliament discussing
the measure in its several effects.
and introducing separate Bills
for each improved suggestion I
am for the Union between England and
Ireland then why not it be the Union
with promoting the best interests of
both that peace and happiness may
prove the result in time to come.
Every office in now very much over
worked so I shall not prolong
my impressive comments. Out of
such minor differences great wars have
arisen; in our affairs let us in future
try and avert them. Back up this Bill
and let it be discussed in Parliament
and the merits or demerits be solved
upon the facts essentially wanting
remedy and you go ahead fearing
no mine shall rock your destiny. I am
Sir
Faithy Yours P. J. Sullivan.
